title: Chapter 7: The CCD Camera
content: On day nine, Chloe received a letter.
It came during morning distribution, handed to her by Derek the orderly in a plain envelope. No return address. But she recognized the handwriting.
Her mother.
*Dear Chloe,*

*We hope you are settling in and taking the program seriously. Dr. Webber tells us you have been moved to Yellow Tier. That is wonderful progress. Keep cooperating, and you will be home before you know it.*
*Your father and I are proud of you for finally making good choices.*
*Love, Mom*
Chloe read it three times.
Then she folded it very carefully and put it in her pocket.
"What is it?" Tiffany asked from across the table.
"My mother's idea of love."
"That bad?"
"Worse. It is the kind of letter that sounds nice if you do not know the context. But I know the context."
She pushed her tray away. The oatmeal suddenly felt heavy in her stomach.
Chloe found Mia in the supply closet, soldering something to the router.
"I need a camera," Chloe said.
Mia did not look up. "There are sixty-three cameras in this facility. You are surrounded by cameras."
"I need my own camera. One they do not control."
Mia paused. Set down the soldering iron. "Why?"
"Because I want to document this place. The real version. Not the brochure version with the lavender-scented hallways and the smiling patients."
"That is dangerous."
"So is staying silent."
Mia considered this. "I might have something. But it is not digital."
"What is it?"
"An old CCD camera. Film. My parents bought it for a 'vintage photography phase' I had when I was fourteen. They confiscated it when they decided the phase was a symptom. But it is still in storage."
"Where is storage?"
"Basement. Room B-17. Locked."
Chloe pulled out the small silver key from her pocket.
"This key. I found it in the birdbath three days ago."
Mia stared at the key. "You found a key in a birdbath that might open a locked basement door, and you did not tell me?"
"I did not know what it opened until now."
That night, Chloe and Mia moved through the darkened corridors.

Mia had mapped the camera blind spots from the router data. They moved in pulses — three seconds of cover, five seconds of movement, pause. Like dancers who had rehearsed the choreography for weeks, not hours.
The basement door was at the end of the east corridor, behind a fire door marked STAFF ONLY.
The key fit.
"Of course it fits," Mia muttered. "A key in a birdbath opens a locked basement door. This is the kind of logic that only exists in movies and broken institutions."
"Or institutions run by people who think they are invincible," Chloe said. "They get sloppy."
Room B-17 was a storage closet. Boxes of old files. Broken furniture. A filing cabinet with a stuck drawer.
And a box labeled M. CHEN — PERSONAL EFFECTS.
Chloe opened it.
Inside was a camera. An old Canon Powershot — the chunky, silver kind from the early 2000s. The kind that ran on AA batteries and stored photos on a SD card small enough to hide anywhere.
A CCD camera. The kind that gave photos that warm, nostalgic glow. The kind that predated surveillance.
"It still has batteries," Chloe whispered.
"I hid fresh ones inside the box before they confiscated it."
"You planned this."
"I planned to document my parents' crimes. I did not know I would need to document this place too."
Chloe held the camera in her hands. It was heavier than she expected. Solid. Real. It could not be traced. It could not be hacked. It was a machine that captured truth without permission.
"Let me see the router data," Chloe said.
Mia pulled out the tablet. "What are you looking for?"
"The hidden server. We know it sends data to my father's firm at 2 AM. But we do not know what it sends."
"Patient data. Financial records. Probably."
"Probably is not enough. I need photographs. Proof. Something a jury can look at."
Mia looked at the camera. Then at Chloe. "You are not planning to escape, are you?"
"No."
"What are you planning?"
Chloe lifted the camera to her eye and pressed the shutter. The click was loud in the silent basement.
"I am planning to make sure they cannot escape us."
Back in her room, Chloe hid the camera under a loose floorboard she had discovered on day two. She lay on her bed and stared at the ceiling.
For the first time since arriving, she felt like she was holding a weapon.
Not a physical one. Something better.
The truth.
And she had the pictures to prove it.
The next morning, Chloe woke to the sound of raised voices in the corridor.
She peered through the crack in her door.
Nurse Cross was standing outside Sarah's room. Sarah's voice was shaking but determined.
"I want to call my lawyer."
"You are not allowed phone calls until you reach Green Tier."
"That is not true. The patient handbook says Yellow Tier patients get one phone call per week."
"The handbook is outdated."
"Then show me the updated version."
Chloe smiled in the dim light. Sarah was finding her spine.
She dressed quickly and stepped into the hallway. "Morning, Nurse Cross. Morning, Sarah."
Nurse Cross turned. Her eyes flicked between them. "You two are becoming quite close."
"Ward bonding. It is in the treatment plan."
"The treatment plan does not include unauthorized meetings."
"Good thing we are authorized patients, then."
Sarah let out a small breath. The tension in her shoulders eased.
Nurse Cross looked at them for a long moment. Then she turned and walked away.
"She is scared of you," Sarah whispered.
"She is scared of us. That is different."
Sarah looked at Chloe with something new in her eyes. Not gratitude. Recognition. "What is your plan?"
"Wait and see."
"I can wait."
"Good. Because when it happens, I am going to need everyone."
That afternoon, Chloe took her first photograph.
It was not of the facility. Not yet. It was of Tiffany eating lunch.

She framed the shot carefully — Tiffany's hands around a ceramic bowl, steam rising, her face relaxed for the first time since Chloe had met her. The CCD sensor captured the warmth of the moment in a way a phone camera never could. Soft. Golden. Human.
"What are you doing?" Tiffany asked.
"Documenting."
"Documenting what?"
"The truth."
Tiffany looked at the camera. Then at Chloe. She did not ask to see the photo. She just went back to eating.
Chloe pressed the shutter again.
She would fill this camera with evidence. Every meal. Every therapy session. Every locked door. Every camera in the ceiling.
And when she had enough, she would show the world what Serenity Peak really was.

She slid the camera back under the floorboard and lay down. The SD card was in her pocket, not in the camera. If they found the camera, they would find nothing. She had learned that from spy movies and internet forums.
*The CCD camera is my weapon,* she thought. *The SD card is my ammunition. And I am not done shooting.*
